skill.name: cookoff
skill.description: Guides AI coding agents from an approved design into parallel implementation trials, then compares results and helps select a winner.
skill.summary: Run parallel implementation trials from one design and compare the results.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command, filesystem, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Review found no executable malware, cryptographic code, prompt injection, or data exfiltration; most high-risk alerts are false positives from prose and examples. The remaining risk is legitimate but material because the skill instructs agents to create worktrees, run shell commands, write files, and remove git branches.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Workflow Requires User Oversight
medium_findings.description: The skill gives agents shell command examples for git worktrees, directory creation, recursive diffs, worktree removal, and branch deletion. These commands are part of the stated development workflow, but they can modify or delete local repository state if used incorrectly.
medium_findings.recommendation: Publish with a warning that users should review generated branch names and cleanup commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain explicit local shell command examples. The commands are legitimate for a coding workflow, but the branch deletion and worktree cleanup commands have real repository impact.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes in Generated Planning Areas
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to work inside per-implementation worktrees and save plans and results under docs/plans. This is expected behavior, but it grants the workflow broad local file modification capability.
medium_findings.recommendation: Require normal workspace boundaries and user review before writing result files or cleaning generated worktrees.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly name worktree directories and output file locations. This is semantically aligned with the skill purpose, but it still modifies user files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited high-risk static alerts do not correspond to cryptographic APIs, weak hashes, ciphers, password handling, or encoded payloads. The lines are skill metadata, workflow prose, and implementation examples.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss the weak cryptography alerts unless future versions add real cryptographic code or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic implementation or credential flow in the cited context. The static hits appear keyword-driven.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations describe test status and result rationale, not network scanning, host discovery, port probing, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss these reconnaissance alerts for this version.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain normal review and reporting language. No network or system reconnaissance command is present there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Destructive Git Cleanup Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The cleanup section includes commands that remove worktrees and force-delete branches. This can be acceptable after user approval, but it is the highest-impact command pattern in the skill.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Require explicit confirmation before running cleanup commands, especially branch deletion.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in the cleanup example and have direct repository deletion effects. The scope is limited to generated cookoff branches.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose quality signals when one agent implements a design alone. This skill runs parallel implementation trials from the same design and guides comparison, review, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Detects when a design is ready for implementation.
Offers single-agent, parallel-agent, and local implementation paths.
Maps feature complexity to a suggested number of implementations.
Defines a worktree-based structure for independent implementation attempts.
Guides review, judging, winner selection, and cleanup.
Preserves plans and result summaries under docs/plans.
limitations: Requires a clear design or implementation goal before use.
Depends on compatible agent and git worktree workflows.
Does not implement code by itself without an execution agent.
Cleanup commands must be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Compare architecture choices
use_cases.description: Run several implementations from one design to compare maintainability, test coverage, and design fit.

use_cases.title: Reduce implementation risk
use_cases.description: Use independent plans and fresh reviews before choosing code for a risky feature.

use_cases.title: Structure agent coding work
use_cases.description: Give Claude, Codex, or Claude Code a repeatable process for parallel coding trials.

prompt_templates.title: Start from an approved design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cookoff for this approved design. Show my implementation options before creating worktrees or dispatching agents.

prompt_templates.title: Run a small comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cookoff with two implementations. Keep both teams focused on the same design and report the differences after tests pass.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate a high-risk feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cookoff for this feature that touches authentication. Spawn independent plans, require TDD, run fresh review, and judge the survivors.

prompt_templates.title: Select and clean up a winner
prompt_templates.prompt: Finish the cookoff by verifying the winner, writing the result summary, and asking before removing losing worktrees or branches.

output_examples.input: Let us build the approved billing settings design.
output_examples.output: The assistant offers implementation choices, recommends a three-way cookoff, and explains why the feature has medium complexity.
The assistant summarizes test results, review findings, design adherence, and the recommended winner without hiding tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Clean up after choosing implementation two.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies the winner, records the result, and asks before removing losing worktrees and branches.

best_practices: Start with a design document or a clear approved scope.
Let each implementation team create its own plan from the same design.
Review cleanup commands before deleting worktrees or branches.
anti_patterns: Do not give every team the same detailed implementation plan.
Do not dispatch parallel teams one at a time.
Do not select a winner using test counts alone.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It organizes parallel implementation attempts from one design and helps compare the results.

faq.question: Does it require git worktrees?
faq.answer: The documented workflow uses worktrees, but a user can adapt the process to another isolated workspace setup.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it includes shell commands and repository cleanup steps.

faq.question: Does it contain malware?
faq.answer: No evidence of malware, exfiltration, prompt injection, or hidden execution was found in the reviewed file.

faq.question: Who should use it?
faq.answer: It is useful for developers and engineering leads who want more than one implementation option.

faq.question: What should users review carefully?
faq.answer: Users should review worktree creation, file writes, and branch deletion commands before execution.
